I think using a recursive approach might be tricky because of the possible nesting. So a stack-based method could be better. Each time I hit '(', I push the current counts and the current multiplier onto the stack. Then, when I hit ')', I process the current group, multiply by the number that follows, and merge it back into the previous state.

Parsing the number:

   parse the element name: j = i+1; while j < len and s[j] is lowercase, j++. element is s.substr(i, j-i).

int i = 0;

while (i < formula.size()) {

   if (isupper(formula[i])) {

      // parse element

      int j = i + 1;

      while (j < formula.size() && islower(formula[j])) {

Parsing the element name:

- Start with an uppercase letter, then include as many lowercase letters as possible.

Parsing the number:

- After the element name or the closing parenthesis, read as many digits as possible. If no digits, the count is 1.

So, in code, for each position i in the string:

if s[i] is uppercase:

   parse the element name: j = i+1; while j < len and s[j] is lowercase, j++. element is s.substr(i, j-i).

   then, parse the number: k = j; while k < len and s[k] is digit, k++. if k == j, count is 1, else count is stoi(s.substr(j, k-j)).

   add to current_map: element -> count.

   i = k-1.

else if s[i] is '(':

   push current_map and current_multiplier (1) to stack.

   current_map.clear();

   current_multiplier = 1;

else if s[i] is ')':

But how to parse the element names and numbers.

Parsing the element name:

- Start at position i, which is an uppercase letter.

- j = i + 1.

- while j < len and s[j] is lowercase, increment j.

- element is s.substr(i, j - i).

Parsing the number:

- after the element, start at j.

- if j >= len, count is 1.

- else, if s[j] is a digit, read all consecutive digits.

- count is the parsed number, default to 1.

Similarly, after a ')', parse the number.

So, in code, I'll need to write functions or code snippets to parse these.

But in the code, I can manage this with indices.

So, the code outline is:

int i = 0;

while (i < formula.size()) {

   if (isupper(formula[i])) {

      // parse element

      int j = i + 1;

      while (j < formula.size() && islower(formula[j])) {

         j++;

      }

      string element = formula.substr(i, j - i);

      // parse number

      int num = 0;

      int k = j;

      while (k < formula.size() && isdigit(formula[k])) {

         k++;

      }

      if (k == j) {

         num = 1;

      } else {

         num = stoi(formula.substr(j, k - j));

      }

      current_map[element] += num;

      i = k;

   } else if (formula[i] == '(') {

      stack.push({current_map, 1});

      current_map.clear();

      i++;

   } else if (formula[i] == ')') {

      // parse number after )

      int j = i + 1;

      while (j < formula.size() && isdigit(formula[j])) {

         j++;

      }

      int group_multiplier = 1;

      if (j > i + 1) {

         group_multiplier = stoi(formula.substr(i+1, j - (i+1)));

      }

      // pop previous map and multiplier

      auto [prev_map, prev_multiplier] = stack.top();

      stack.pop();

      // multiply current_map by group_multiplier

      for (auto& pair : current_map) {

         pair.second *= group_multiplier;

      }

      // add to prev_map, multiplied by prev_multiplier

      for (const auto& pair : current_map) {

         prev_map[pair.first] += pair.second * prev_multiplier;

      }

      current_map = prev_map;

      i = j;

   }

}

Now, after processing the entire string, the current_map holds all the counts.

Then, we need to sort the elements lexicographically and build the output string.

So, extract all the elements from current_map, sort them, and then for each, append the element name and count (if count > 1).

So, in code:

vector<string> elements;

for (const auto& pair : current_map) {

   elements.push_back(pair.first);

}

sort(elements.begin(), elements.end());

string result;

for (const auto& elem : elements) {

   result += elem;

   int count = current_map[elem];

   if (count > 1) {

      result += to_string(count);

   }

}

return result;
